talk_title: Molecular mechanisms of differentiation therapy and arsenic in acute promyelocytic leukemia

clinical_data.population: APL patients treated with arsenic alone

MOA_summary: ATRA binds the RARα moiety of the PML‑RARα fusion, converting it from a transcriptional repressor to an activator, while arsenic binds a cysteine triad in the PML moiety, inducing SUMO‑dependent ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of PML‑RARα and restoration of PML nuclear bodies, leading to p53 activation, senescence, and terminal differentiation of leukemic promyelocytes.
key_findings: ATRA alone induces differentiation but rarely cures APL; arsenic alone cures ~2/3 of patients; the combination cures virtually all patients.
Both agents directly degrade the PML‑RARα oncoprotein via distinct ubiquitin‑proteasome pathways (UBR5 for ATRA, SUMO‑dependent ligase for arsenic).
Restoration of PML nuclear bodies after treatment correlates with p53 activation and loss of self‑renewal, essential for cure in mouse models.
Mutations in the arsenic‑binding cysteine triad confer resistance, confirming the binding site’s importance.
PML is required for response to conventional chemotherapy in non‑APL leukemias, indicating a broader role in therapy response.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Arsenic‑resistant APL patients harbor mutations in the PML cysteine triad that prevent arsenic binding.
FLT3 activation influences retinoic acid response but not the ATRA‑arsenic combination.

combination_strategies.rationale: Both agents degrade PML‑RARα via complementary mechanisms, synergizing to restore nuclear bodies and activate p53.
combination_strategies.phenotype: dramatic tumor burden reduction and cure in mouse models; near‑100% cure in patients

open_questions: What downstream retinoic acid target genes enforce self‑renewal in APL?
Can the PML‑senescence hub be exploited to sensitize other cancers to therapy?

cited_papers: Lococo et al. historical APL trial

talk_title: Apoptotic priming as a determinant of chemotherapy sensitivity across solid and liquid cancers

biomarkers.name: BH3 profiling priming score
biomarkers.finding: Higher priming correlates with faster CA125 normalization and improved PFS in ovarian cancer

models.name: DepMap panel
models.genotype_or_key_features: various solid and hematologic cancer lines

models.name: CLL, follicular lymphoma, AML primary specimens
pediatric rhabdomyosarcoma (neonatal vs adult satellite cells)

clinical_data.value: significant in pediatric rhabdomyosarcoma, none in adult

MOA_summary: Differential apoptotic priming, measured by BH3 profiling, determines how readily cancer cells undergo mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization upon chemotherapy; hematologic and pediatric CNS cancers are highly primed and thus chemo‑sensitive, whereas most solid tumors are unprimed and require inhibition of multiple BCL‑2 family members to trigger death.
key_findings: Blood cancers and testicular germ cell tumors are intrinsically more primed for apoptosis than most solid tumors, correlating with clinical chemosensitivity.
Baseline BH3 profiling priming scores predict faster CA125 normalization and longer PFS in ovarian cancer patients.
CRISPR knockout of Bax/Bak in HeLa cells reduces priming and confers broad chemoresistance, confirming causality.
Solid tumor cell lines require simultaneous inhibition of multiple pro‑survival BCL‑2 proteins (BCL‑XL, MCL‑1) for cell death, unlike hematologic lines.
Neonatal muscle‑derived rhabdomyosarcomas retain higher Bax/Bim levels, are more primed, and respond better to vincristine; BCL‑XL degrader DT2216 synergizes to improve response.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Adult rhabdomyosarcoma shows low priming and resistance to vincristine alone.
BCL‑2 family redundancy (upregulation of MCL‑1/BCL‑XL) limits efficacy of single BCL‑2 inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Sensitize low‑primed tumors by removing a key pro‑survival protein
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced vincristine response in adult rhabdomyosarcoma mouse model

open_questions: What molecular determinants beyond cell‑of‑origin set the basal priming level in different tissues?
Can dynamic changes in priming after radiation or targeted therapy be leveraged for adaptive treatment scheduling?

talk_title: NF1 as a microtubule‑repair factor and predictor of maytansinoid ADC efficacy in HER2‑positive breast cancer

biomarkers.name: NF1 mutation status
biomarkers.finding: NF1‑mutated patients showed significantly longer progression‑free survival on T‑DM1 (p<0.01, n=34)

models.name: HER2‑positive breast cancer lines (WT and NF1‑KO)
NSG mice bearing WT or NF1‑KO tumors
34 HER2‑positive breast cancer patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: NF1 mutation vs wild‑type, treated with T‑DM1

models.name: neonatal vs adult muscle satellite cell‑derived rhabdomyosarcoma

clinical_data.population: NF1‑mutated HER2‑positive breast cancer patients on T‑DM1

MOA_summary: NF1 binds directly to polymerized microtubules, accelerates tubulin polymerization and promotes self‑repair of intra‑microtubule damage; loss of NF1 impairs this repair, creating more drug‑binding sites for maytansinoid payloads (DM1) on exposed protofilament ends, thereby sensitizing NF1‑deficient cells to T‑DM1 ADCs.
readouts: co‑sedimentation and EM of NF1‑microtubule complexes
thermal shift assay for DM1 binding
key_findings: NF1 loss does NOT affect Ras signaling in this context but dramatically increases sensitivity to the maytansinoid ADC T‑DM1.
Re‑expression of wild‑type NF1 rescues mitotic timing defects and reduces DM1 sensitivity, confirming specificity.
NF1 directly binds microtubules and accelerates polymerization; in vitro, NF1 counteracts DM1‑induced polymerization block.
NF1‑deficient cells display impaired microtubule self‑repair, leading to increased DM1 binding sites and heightened cytotoxicity.
Clinical cohort analysis shows NF1‑mutated HER2‑positive breast cancer patients have significantly longer PFS on T‑DM1.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NF1 loss creates a vulnerability to maytansinoid payloads but may also increase genomic instability (higher aneuploidy scores).
Ras‑GAP deficient NF1 mutants still bind microtubules but fail to promote repair, separating Ras and microtubule functions.

combination_strategies.rationale: To determine if the payload or antibody component drives differential sensitivity
combination_strategies.phenotype: No differential effect; sensitivity linked to DM1 payload

open_questions: Which NF1 domains mediate microtubule binding versus Ras GAP activity?
Can NF1 expression levels (beyond mutation) serve as a biomarker for maytansinoid ADC response?
Does p53 status modulate the relative contribution of NF1’s Ras versus microtubule functions in therapy response?

patent_or_ip_hints: Potential IP on NF1‑microtubule interaction modulators
